items.q: How does myelination increase the speed of nerve impulse conduction?
items.correct: Action potentials jump between nodes of Ranvier (saltatory conduction), skipping the myelinated sections.
items.misconception: Myelin makes the axon thicker so the signal travels faster through more material.
items.random_wrong: Myelin contains additional ion channels that speed up depolarisation.
items.why: Students think 'more material = faster' rather than understanding that myelin INSULATES so the impulse can leap between gaps (saltatory).

items.q: Why are enzymes specific to certain substrates?
items.correct: Only substrates with complementary shape and chemistry to the active site can bind effectively.
items.misconception: Each enzyme can react with any substrate, but works fastest with one type.
items.random_wrong: Enzymes choose their substrate based on which one is most common in the cell.
items.why: Students underestimate how strict the lock-and-key/induced-fit relationship is; they think specificity is a preference rather than a structural requirement.

items.q: How does sweating help cool you down?
items.correct: Evaporation of sweat absorbs energy (latent heat) from the skin, lowering body temperature.
items.misconception: Sweat is cold and directly cools the skin on contact.
items.random_wrong: Sweating removes excess heat by carrying it out as liquid waste.
items.why: Students don't connect sweating to evaporation; they think the liquid itself is the cooling agent, not the phase change.
The answer examiners want is: Glycosidic. “Peptide” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Addition of water. “Loss of water” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
